scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Before we talk about how AI can help you in the classroom, we need to agree on what AI actually <emphasis>is</emphasis>. [pause] And — just as importantly — what it isn't.

scenes.name: Clear the noise
scenes.text: Because misconceptions about AI are everywhere. In the news. In the staffroom. In the way products are marketed to you. [pause] Some make us afraid. Others promise too much. [pause] Neither is the truth.

scenes.name: Definition
scenes.text: So — at its core, artificial intelligence is a field of computer science. [pause] It builds systems that can do things we usually associate with human thinking: <emphasis>recognising patterns</emphasis>, <emphasis>understanding language</emphasis>, <emphasis>making decisions</emphasis>, <emphasis>generating content</emphasis>. [pause] Tasks we used to think only people could do.

scenes.name: Key insight
scenes.text: But here's the part most people get wrong. [pause] AI does not actually <emphasis>think</emphasis>. [pause] What it does is process statistical patterns — learned from enormous amounts of data. [pause] No understanding. No intention. No consciousness. [pause] Just very, very good pattern-matching.

scenes.name: What AI can do
scenes.text: And that pattern-matching is genuinely useful. [pause] It can recognise patterns in text, images, and data. Generate human-like writing in dozens of languages. Translate in real time. Summarise long documents into the gist. Answer questions based on what it learned in training. [pause] All of which can save you real hours on real tasks.

scenes.name: What AI cannot do
scenes.text: Now — equally important — what it <emphasis>cannot</emphasis> do. [pause] It cannot truly understand meaning the way you do. It cannot feel anything, even when its writing sounds opinionated. It cannot guarantee it's telling the truth — it hallucinates, confidently. [pause] And it cannot replace your judgment. The things that make you a great teacher are precisely the things AI can't do.

scenes.name: Three myths
scenes.text: Three myths worth retiring right now. [pause] <emphasis>AI knows everything.</emphasis> It doesn't — it invents, confidently. [pause] <emphasis>AI will replace teachers.</emphasis> It won't — every technology predicted to replace us has only changed <emphasis>how</emphasis> we teach. [pause] <emphasis>AI understands what I'm saying.</emphasis> It doesn't — it's matching patterns. That is not the same as comprehension.

scenes.name: Chatbot vs LLM
scenes.text: One quick distinction. [pause] When people say AI, they usually mean a chatbot — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini. [pause] But the chatbot is just the interface. The actual AI is the <emphasis>large language model</emphasis> behind it. [pause] The chatbot is the phone. The model is the voice on the other end.

scenes.name: Narrow AI vs AGI
scenes.text: And every tool you use today is a <emphasis>specialist</emphasis>. [pause] ChatGPT can write, but it can't drive a car. An image model can see, but it can't reason. [pause] A single AI that does <emphasis>everything</emphasis> a human can — doesn't exist yet. All of today's AI is narrow.

scenes.name: Already in education
scenes.text: And here's the thing — AI has been in your classroom for years. [pause] Khan Academy, Duolingo, Google Translate, Grammarly, auto-graders, YouTube recommendations. [pause] You've been using it all along.

scenes.name: You already have the skills
scenes.text: Which brings us to the good news. [pause] You already have the skills this takes. [pause] Writing a clear lesson plan is the same muscle as writing a clear prompt. Scaffolding a task for a student is exactly how you get good AI output. Grading a paper is evaluating an output. And iterating when a lesson flops — that's how you work with AI, too.
